title: State Historic Preservation Office

infobox.value: (**SHPO**) is a [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") or territorial governmental function described by the United States federal government in 1966 under Section 101 of the [National Historic Preservation Act](/wiki/National_Historic_Preservation_Act "National Historic Preservation Act") (NHPA).[[1]](#cite_note-1) As described in that law, the purposes of a SHPO include surveying and recognizing historic properties, reviewing nominations for properties to be included in the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"), reviewing undertakings for the impact on the properties as well as supporting federal organizations, [state](/wiki/State_government "State government") and [local governments](/wiki/Local_government "Local government"), and private sector.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Federal law cannot force a state to create a SHPO, but to obtain several benefits under the law, a state needs to do so (or designate one of its existing agencies for these tasks). As each state is responsible for setting up their own SHPO, each SHPO may vary in [rules](/wiki/Law "Law") and [regulations](/wiki/Regulations "Regulations"). To link these differences with the SHPOs, the [National Conference of State Historic Preservation Officers](/wiki/National_Conference_of_State_Historic_Preservation_Officers "National Conference of State Historic Preservation Officers") (NCSHPO)[[4]](#cite_note-4) was created as a "point of contact" according to the [National Historic Preservation Act](/wiki/National_Historic_Preservation_Act "National Historic Preservation Act").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

State function aimed at preserving historical sites

The **State Historic Preservation Office** (**SHPO**) is a [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") or territorial governmental function described by the United States federal government in 1966 under Section 101 of the [National Historic Preservation Act](/wiki/National_Historic_Preservation_Act "National Historic Preservation Act") (NHPA).[[1]](#cite_note-1) As described in that law, the purposes of a SHPO include surveying and recognizing historic properties, reviewing nominations for properties to be included in the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"), reviewing undertakings for the impact on the properties as well as supporting federal organizations, [state](/wiki/State_government "State government") and [local governments](/wiki/Local_government "Local government"), and private sector.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Federal law cannot force a state to create a SHPO, but to obtain several benefits under the law, a state needs to do so (or designate one of its existing agencies for these tasks). As each state is responsible for setting up their own SHPO, each SHPO may vary in [rules](/wiki/Law "Law") and [regulations](/wiki/Regulations "Regulations"). To link these differences with the SHPOs, the [National Conference of State Historic Preservation Officers](/wiki/National_Conference_of_State_Historic_Preservation_Officers "National Conference of State Historic Preservation Officers") (NCSHPO)[[4]](#cite_note-4) was created as a "point of contact" according to the [National Historic Preservation Act](/wiki/National_Historic_Preservation_Act "National Historic Preservation Act").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_Historic_Preservation_Office&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

In 1966, the [National Historic Preservation Act](/wiki/National_Historic_Preservation_Act "National Historic Preservation Act") (NHPA) was put into effect. As part of the [Congressional Act](/wiki/Congressional_Act "Congressional Act"), Section 101 implemented the designation of the State Historic Preservation Program. State Liaison Officers, which later became known as State Historic Preservation Officers, were established to manage historic preservation grants for the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") (NPS). In the 1970s, these SHPOs experienced a growth in power as they became more organized, efficient and professional, and clarified their relationships with NPS.

They also formed a [National Conference of State Historic Preservation Officers](/wiki/National_Conference_of_State_Historic_Preservation_Officers "National Conference of State Historic Preservation Officers") to represent them on a National level, particularly in Washington. The SHPO continued to gain an increasingly specific role, taking on the position of the advising consultant for the Section 106 review process. In 1980 with the amendment to the NHPA, the SHPO's exact duties were finally identified, defining its role, which remains today.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Purposes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_Historic_Preservation_Office&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Purposes")]

The responsibilities of the State Historic Preservation Office, according to the [National Historic Preservation Act](/wiki/National_Historic_Preservation_Act "National Historic Preservation Act") of 1966, as amended, include running the State Historic Preservation Program and, as stated in the Act:

1. In cooperation with [Federal](/wiki/List_of_United_States_federal_agencies "List of United States federal agencies") and [State agencies](/wiki/State_agency "State agency"), [local governments](/wiki/Local_government "Local government"), and [private organizations](/wiki/Non-governmental_organization "Non-governmental organization") and individuals, direct and conduct a comprehensive statewide survey of historic properties and maintain inventories of such properties;
2. Identify and nominate eligible [properties](/wiki/Property_type_(National_Register_of_Historic_Places) "Property type (National Register of Historic Places)") to the [National Register](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") and otherwise administer applications for listing historic properties on the National register;
3. Prepare and implement a comprehensive statewide [historic preservation](/wiki/Historic_preservation "Historic preservation") plan;
4. Administer the State program of Federal assistance for historic preservation within the State;
5. Advise and assist, as appropriate, Federal and State agencies and local governments in carrying out their historic preservation responsibilities;
6. Cooperate with the [Secretary](/wiki/Secretary "Secretary"), the [Advisory Council on Historic Preservation](/wiki/Advisory_Council_on_Historic_Preservation "Advisory Council on Historic Preservation"), and other Federal and State agencies, local governments, and organizations and individuals to ensure historic properties are taken into consideration at all levels of planning and development;
   }Provide public information, education and training, and technical assistance relating to the Federal and State Historic Preservation Programs; and
7. Cooperate with local governments in the development of the local historic preservation programs and assist local governments in becoming certified pursuant to subsection (C).[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Definitions
"*Comprehensive Statewide Historic preservation plan*" – The official plan assumed by local governments throughout the state, that guides decision making over proposed public and private actions affecting community development.[[8]](#cite_note-8) View an example [here](http://nysparks.state.ny.us/shpo/).

"*Secretary*" refers to the [Secretary of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior") in the [Department of the Interior](/wiki/Department_of_the_Interior "Department of the Interior").

"*Historic Preservation Program*" – A program designed to support and promote historic preservation interests and priorities. There are National, Federal and State Preservation Programs, each tailored to suit the needs at each particular level.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

"*Certified local government*"- For a city or town's local government to become certified, it has to meet particular standards so that it can participate in certain NHPA programs.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## SHPO among states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_Historic_Preservation_Office&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: SHPO among states")]

SHPOs exist not only in [50 states](/wiki/50_states "50 states"), but also in the [District of Columbia](/wiki/District_of_Columbia "District of Columbia"), [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa "American Samoa"), the [Virgin Islands](/wiki/Virgin_Islands "Virgin Islands"), [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam"), the [Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands](/wiki/Northern_Mariana_Islands "Northern Mariana Islands"), the [Republic of Palau](/wiki/Republic_of_Palau "Republic of Palau"), the [Republic of Marshall Islands](/wiki/Marshall_Islands "Marshall Islands"), and the [Federated States of Micronesia](/wiki/Federated_States_of_Micronesia "Federated States of Micronesia"); making 59 SHPOs total.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Each State Historic Preservation Office is responsible for effective planning to address preservation matters.[[12]](#cite_note-12) For example, [agricultural](/wiki/Agricultural "Agricultural") [structures](/wiki/Structures "Structures") such as [barns](/wiki/Barn "Barn") are significant to [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire")'s "values of heritage, hard work, productivity and stewardship" and the state has devoted special programs to help preserve these values.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In Florida, [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") buildings in [South Beach](/wiki/South_Beach "South Beach") are considered significant to the state.[[14]](#cite_note-14) As a result of these differences, states have expanded and/or specialized their SHPO purposes and created private organizations. Alaska's Coastal Management Program is a private organization, created by the state to regulate agencies to acknowledge environmental and cultural resources within the Alaskan Coast and protect them.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## THPO

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_Historic_Preservation_Office&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: THPO")]

Main article: [Tribal Historic Preservation Officer](/wiki/Tribal_Historic_Preservation_Officer "Tribal Historic Preservation Officer")

[Tribal Historic Preservation Officers](/wiki/Tribal_Historic_Preservation_Officer "Tribal Historic Preservation Officer") (THPOs) assume "some or all of the functions of State Historic Preservation Officers on [Tribal lands](/wiki/Tribal_sovereignty_in_the_United_States "Tribal sovereignty in the United States")."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## NCSHPO

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_Historic_Preservation_Office&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: NCSHPO")]

The National Conference of State Historic Preservation Officers (NCSHPO) is a non-profit organization for State Historic Preservation Officers (SHPO). It serves as a way of contacting the SHPOs as well as a way for the SHPOs to communicate with each other.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The mission of the NCSHPO is to act as a national representative for the SHPOs, especially when it comes to representing their interests in Washington.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The NCSHPO meets once a year and participants include not only SHPOs, but members of [Preservation Action](/wiki/Preservation_Action "Preservation Action") and the [National Alliance of Preservation Commissions](/w/index.php?title=National_Alliance_of_Preservation_Commissions&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Alliance of Preservation Commissions (page does not exist)"), [National Trust for Historic Preservation Advisors](/w/index.php?title=National_Trust_for_Historic_Preservation_Advisors&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Trust for Historic Preservation Advisors (page does not exist)") and federal agencies. Participants are updated on important [legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative") issues and then visit [Capitol Hill](/wiki/Capitol_Hill "Capitol Hill") with the information, to meet with their [Representatives](/wiki/United_States_Congressman "United States Congressman") and [Senators](/wiki/United_States_Senator "United States Senator") to establish favorable positions on these issues.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Generally the president of the NCSHPO is a previous member of the [Advisory Council on Historic Preservation](/wiki/Advisory_Council_on_Historic_Preservation "Advisory Council on Historic Preservation") (ACHP) while the Board of Directors, which governs NCSHPO, is elected by the member states and meets more frequently than once a year. It acts as a representative for them with federal agencies and national preservation organizations. It is important to have a close relationship with these federal agencies and national organizations, and NCSHPO maintains these by partnering closely with organizations such as: The ACHP, The [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service"), The [National Trust for Historic Preservation](/wiki/National_Trust_for_Historic_Preservation "National Trust for Historic Preservation") and [Preservation Action](/wiki/Preservation_Action "Preservation Action").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=State_Historic_Preservation_Office&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Save America's Treasures](/wiki/Save_America%27s_Treasures "Save America's Treasures")
* [Tribal Historic Preservation Officer](/wiki/Tribal_Historic_Preservation_Officer "Tribal Historic Preservation Officer")
